title: Kinematical correlations via $κ$-Poincaré coproducts
abstract: We study a kinematical consequence of the Hopf-algebraic momentum composition law in $κ$-Minkowski spacetime. The same curved momentum space can be described in different coordinates. In the bicrossproduct basis the ordered-plane-wave labels are the translation-generator eigenvalues, so the relevant map is one-to-one. In the classical basis, instead, the translation eigenvalues $P_μ$ are nonlinearly related to the ordered-plane-wave labels $p_μ$. This relation can fail to be globally one-to-one in a high-momentum region. When a given classical-basis four-momentum admits more than one real auxiliary preimage, the branch-sensitive quantity $P_+\equiv P_0+P_4=κe^{p_0/κ}$ enters the coproduct and resolves the branches in two-particle states. Imposing the vanishing total-momentum constraint therefore gives branch-dependent $κ$-deformed back-to-back momentum correlations. In a single-branch regime this is just a deformed correlated product, while in a multibranch regime a state specified only by $P_μ$ can be expanded into distinct auxiliary branches. If $P_μ$ are taken as the directly meaningful momenta, the physical content is the resulting deformed correlation pattern. If the auxiliary variables $p_μ$ are assigned operational meaning, the same constrained state can be interpreted as a superposition over different auxiliary branches. We also compare this structure with standard regular self-adjoint nonrelativistic minimal-length models and find no analogous smooth local two-real-branch inversion on their physical domains.
summary: This theoretical paper explores the kinematics of two-particle states in $\kappa$-Minkowski spacetime, a model incorporating quantum gravity effects via a deformation scale $\kappa$. The core finding is that the momentum composition law forces the appearance of distinct, branch-dependent correlations when the system enters a high-momentum regime. This mechanism is shown to be mathematically distinct from simpler nonrelativistic quantum models.
main_problem: The paper investigates the kinematical consequences of the Hopf-algebraic momentum composition law within $\kappa$-Minkowski spacetime, focusing on how this structure dictates two-particle momentum correlations.
main_result: The structure leads to branch-dependent $\kappa$-deformed back-to-back momentum correlations in the high-momentum regime ($|\vec{P}| > \kappa$), which is a unique feature distinguishing it from standard nonrelativistic minimal-length models.
method: The analysis involves comparing two parameterizations of the curved momentum space (ordered-plane-wave labels $p_\mu$ vs. classical-basis momenta $P_\mu$) and analyzing the resulting nonlinear map and its associated coproduct structure.
model_or_system: The system is $\kappa$-Minkowski spacetime, governed by the $\kappa$-Poincaré Hopf algebra, which introduces a deformation scale $\kappa$ related to the Planck energy. The study focuses on the two-particle state structure derived from the coproduct constraint.
key_observables:
Deformed back-to-back momentum correlations, the branch-sensitive quantity $P^+ \equiv P_0 + P_4$, and the Jacobian determinant $\det \partial P_\mu / \partial p_
u$.
important_parameters: Deformation scale $\kappa$, the classical four-momentum $P_\mu$, and the ordered-plane-wave labels $p_\mu$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the existence of a regime where the inverse map $P 	o p$ is multi-valued (the two-branch regime), and it contrasts this with standard nonrelativistic models that lack this specific type of local inversion.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the function $f$ and the graphical solution of the inverse map, showing the transition from a single-valued to a two-branch structure as the momentum magnitude $|\vec{P}|$ crosses the threshold $\kappa$.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares the bicrossproduct basis (one-to-one map) with the classical basis (nonlinear map), derives the branch-resolving nature of the coproduct in the classical basis, and concludes by contrasting this structure with nonrelativistic models.
why_interesting: While not directly related to AMO, the mathematical techniques used—analyzing non-linear mappings, branch cuts, and symmetry deformations—are highly relevant to understanding effective Hamiltonians in strongly coupled or highly constrained quantum systems.
